original_abstract:
Clouds play a pivotal role in reshaping the chemical landscape of atmospheric aerosols by serving as a dynamic medium for gas-to-particle interactions. To better understand these cloud-processing mechanisms and characterize the composition of atmospheric organic matter (AOM) within droplets, we analyzed two supercooled cloud samples collected at the Storm Peak Laboratory (3220 m a.s.l.) near Steamboat Springs, Colorado. Following reversed-phase extraction to isolate the organic fraction, ultrahigh-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry revealed approximately 3000 distinct molecular formulas. The chemical profiles were dominated by oxygen-containing (CHO), nitrogen-containing (CHNO), and sulfur-containing (CHOS and CHNOS) compounds with molecular weights reaching 700 Da.

We observed average oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) ratios of ~0.6, suggesting a more oxidized state than typically found in water-soluble organic carbon from aerosol studies—a likely signature of aqueous-phase oxidation. The molecular signatures indicate that the AOM was heavily influenced by biogenic secondary organic aerosol (SOA) and residential wood combustion; notably, 60% of the CHO formulas matched those produced during the ozonolysis of α-pinene, β-pinene, d-limonene, and β-caryophyllene. CHNO compounds showed the highest abundance and diversity, linked primarily to wood smoke and $NO_x$-driven oxidation. In our nighttime samples, the presence of multi-nitrogen formulas underscores the impact of nitrate radical chemistry on night-cloud composition. Additionally, we identified several CHOS and CHNOS species containing reduced sulfur; while their specific origins require further study, their presence adds a layer of complexity to the sulfur cycle in clouds. These findings provide an unambiguous molecular map of organic anions in Rocky Mountain cloud water, offering critical insights to refine our understanding of aqueous-phase atmospheric processing.
humanized_abstract:
Clouds are an important environmental matrix affecting the composition of aerosol chemicals through heterogeneous gas-to-particle transformations. Understanding cloud-processing processes and identifying the organic components within cloud droplets remains an unmet challenge to aerosol science. To this end, we investigated two supercooled cloud samples collected at the Storm Peak Laboratory (3220 m a.s.l.) about 30 km north-northwest of Steamboat Springs, Colorado. After removing the non-organic components using reversed-phase extraction, the resulting dissolved organic carbon (DOC) was subsequently analyzed using ultrahigh-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry. A total of about 3000 individual molecular formulas were identified, which described a DOC mixture primarily composed of oxygen-containing species (CHO), nitrogen-containing species (CHNO), and sulfur-containing species (CHOS and CHNOS) ranging from up to 700 Da in mass.

Using a hybrid quadrupole-Orbitrap mass spectrometer, the oxygen-to-carbon (O/C) ratios for the average formula of OAC were determined to be ~0.6, higher than those previously reported for water-soluble organic carbon (WSOC) in aerosol studies and within the expected range for aqueous-phase oxidation products. The AOM was dominated by biogenic secondary organic aerosol (SOA), residential wood combustion, and other unrelated sources, with 60% of the CHO formulas identified corresponding to known products of the ozonolysis of α-pinene, β-pinene, d-limonene, and β-caryophyllene. The largest pool and diversity of species were attributed to the CHNO class, which are also known to be a large fraction of the WSOC in biomass burning aerosol and $NO_x$-driven oxidation products. The high concentration of nighttime species containing multi-nitrogen formulas suggested that nitrate radical chemistry plays an important role in the formation of the night-cloud. Several CHOS and CHNOS species were identified that contained reduced sulfur in the form of reduced functional groups. Source attribution for these species requires additional analysis, but the presence of sulfur-containing organic species suggests that there may be additional pathways by which sulfur compounds are cycled within clouds, and thus the identification of these compounds provides a more detailed molecular map of the organic anions present in cloud water and underscores the need to refine our understanding of aqueous-phase atmospheric processes.

document.output:
Clouds are an important environmental matrix affecting the composition of aerosol chemicals through heterogeneous gas-to-particle transformations. Understanding cloud-processing processes and identifying the organic components within cloud droplets remains an unmet challenge to aerosol science. document.input:
Clouds play a pivotal role in reshaping the chemical landscape of atmospheric aerosols by serving as a dynamic medium for gas-to-particle interactions.
